hook.text: A CEO earns more in an hour than a janitor earns in a month. Both work full time. Stratification explains why.

buildup.text: Social stratification is the ranking of people into hierarchies based on wealth, power, and prestige.

discovery.text: It's not random. Every known society has some system of ranking. The criteria change, but hierarchy persists.

twist.text: Stratification feels natural to those on top. But the rankings are socially constructed, not biologically given.

climax.text: The system reproduces itself across generations. Where you start largely determines where you end up.

punchline.text: Inequality isn't an accident. It's a system.

quiz.question: What is social stratification?
options: Ranking people by wealth, power, and prestige
A random distribution of resources
A system based purely on individual merit

hook.text: In India, your birth once locked your job for life. In America, anyone can make it. Both stories oversimplify.

buildup.text: Caste systems assign status at birth. You can't change your caste no matter what you achieve.

discovery.text: Class systems are theoretically open. Status is based on achievement, income, and education rather than birth.

twist.text: In practice, class systems often mimic caste. Parental wealth predicts children's outcomes more than effort does.

climax.text: The difference between caste and class is real but smaller than most people assume. Both constrain life chances.

punchline.text: Open systems still have locked doors. They're just harder to see.

quiz.question: How do caste and class systems differ?
options: Caste is fixed at birth; class is theoretically open
Caste is based on wealth; class is based on religion
There is no meaningful difference

hook.text: Twelve million Africans were shipped across the Atlantic in chains. Slavery was stratification at its most extreme.

buildup.text: Slavery is the most rigid form of stratification: one group legally owns another as property.

discovery.text: It existed in ancient Rome, West Africa, and the Americas. The Atlantic system was uniquely racial and industrial.

twist.text: Abolition ended legal slavery, but its economic legacy persists. Wealth gaps created by slavery still shape outcomes.

climax.text: Slavery didn't vanish. It transformed into sharecropping, convict leasing, and systemic racial inequality.

punchline.text: Slavery ended on paper. Its economic shadow never left.

quiz.question: Why does the legacy of slavery still affect stratification?
options: Wealth gaps created by slavery persist across generations
Slavery had no long-term economic effects
All effects ended with abolition

hook.text: A doctor and a plumber earn similar salaries. But mention their jobs at a party and the reactions differ completely.

buildup.text: Prestige is the social respect attached to positions. It doesn't always match income but always shapes treatment.

discovery.text: Status symbols — designer clothes, luxury cars, degrees — signal position. People read them instantly.

twist.text: Veblen called it conspicuous consumption: spending to display status, not to meet needs. It persists everywhere.

climax.text: Prestige hierarchies vary by culture. A warrior's honor, a scholar's rank, a CEO's bonus — all serve the same function.

punchline.text: What you own tells the world where you stand.

quiz.question: What is conspicuous consumption?
options: Spending to display social status rather than meet needs
Buying only essential items
A government economic policy

hook.text: A wealthy Black woman and a poor white man face different barriers. Neither race nor class alone explains their lives.

buildup.text: Kimberlé Crenshaw coined intersectionality in 1989 to describe how race, gender, and class overlap.

discovery.text: A Black woman's experience isn't just racism plus sexism. The combination creates unique disadvantages neither captures.

twist.text: Early feminism centered white women. Early civil rights centered Black men. Intersectionality filled the gap.

climax.text: Stratification is never one-dimensional. People occupy multiple positions that combine in complex, specific ways.

punchline.text: Inequality isn't one ladder. It's a web of overlapping ones.

quiz.question: What does intersectionality describe?
options: How race, gender, and class overlap to create unique experiences
A theory about road infrastructure
The intersection of two academic disciplines

hook.text: Ask Americans their class and most say middle. Ask sociologists and they'll point to your income, education, and job.

buildup.text: Social class combines economic position, educational attainment, and occupational prestige into one ranking.

discovery.text: Marx saw two classes: owners and workers. Weber added a third dimension — status and political power.

twist.text: Most people misidentify their own class. The rich understate; the poor overstate. Self-perception is unreliable.

climax.text: Class shapes nearly everything: health, relationships, tastes, politics, and even how long you live.

punchline.text: Class isn't just money. It's the life money builds.

quiz.question: What factors define social class?
options: Income, education, and occupational prestige
Only annual income
Political party membership

hook.text: The richest 1% own more wealth than the bottom 50% combined. Their money shapes policy, media, and markets.

buildup.text: The upper class includes old money families and new billionaires. Both hold outsized influence on society.

discovery.text: Old money families maintain power through trusts, elite schools, and social networks that exclude outsiders.

twist.text: New wealth is celebrated publicly but often rejected by old elites. Money alone doesn't buy social acceptance.

climax.text: Upper-class influence extends beyond economics. They shape culture, law, and politics to protect their position.

punchline.text: The richest don't just have more. They shape the rules.

quiz.question: How do old money families maintain their status?
options: Through trusts, elite schools, and exclusive networks
By working longer hours than everyone else
Through government welfare programs

hook.text: Your parents bought a house at 30 with one salary. You're 35, dual income, and still renting. The middle is shrinking.

buildup.text: The middle class once defined American life: stable jobs, homeownership, and upward mobility for children.

discovery.text: Since the 1970s, wages stagnated while costs for housing, healthcare, and education skyrocketed.

twist.text: Productivity doubled since 1980, but workers' pay barely moved. The gains went to shareholders and executives.

climax.text: The middle class isn't vanishing equally. Some rose into comfort; more slid toward economic precarity.

punchline.text: The middle class didn't fail. The economy moved without it.

quiz.question: What has happened to the middle class since the 1970s?
options: Wages stagnated while living costs rose sharply
It expanded and grew wealthier
It was abolished by government policy

hook.text: He builds houses he'll never afford to live in. She cleans offices she'll never work in. That's the working class.

buildup.text: The working class performs manual or service labor, often without college degrees, with limited job security.

discovery.text: Unions once gave workers bargaining power. Since the 1980s, union membership has declined from 35% to under 11%.

twist.text: Working-class jobs were once pathways to the middle class. Deregulation and automation closed many of those doors.

climax.text: The working class remains essential. Pandemics proved that society collapses without drivers, cleaners, and nurses.

punchline.text: Essential workers are valued in crisis, forgotten in budgets.

quiz.question: What happened to union membership since the 1980s?
options: It declined sharply from 35% to under 11%
It doubled in size
It stayed roughly the same

hook.text: Are the very poor trapped by culture or by structure? This question has split sociologists for decades.

buildup.text: The underclass refers to the persistently poor: long-term unemployed, often concentrated in segregated neighborhoods.

discovery.text: Oscar Lewis proposed a culture of poverty: values that trap families across generations in self-defeating patterns.

twist.text: William Julius Wilson countered: deindustrialization destroyed jobs. No culture shift causes mass joblessness.

climax.text: The debate matters because the answer shapes policy: job programs vs. behavior interventions target different causes.

punchline.text: Blaming culture is easier. Fixing structures is harder — and more effective.

quiz.question: What did William Julius Wilson argue about persistent poverty?
options: Deindustrialization destroyed jobs and created the underclass
A culture of poverty traps families
Poverty is entirely a personal choice

hook.text: A doctor earning $300k with $500k in student debt is income-rich but wealth-poor. The distinction matters enormously.

buildup.text: Income is what you earn. Wealth is what you own minus what you owe. They measure different things entirely.

discovery.text: Wealth inequality dwarfs income inequality. The top 10% hold over 70% of all wealth in the United States.

twist.text: Wealth passes between generations. Income doesn't. That's why wealth gaps persist even when incomes converge.

climax.text: Policies targeting income miss the bigger story. Without addressing wealth, inequality reproduces itself endlessly.

punchline.text: Income is what you earn today. Wealth decides tomorrow.

quiz.question: Why is wealth inequality more persistent than income inequality?
options: Wealth passes between generations while income doesn't
Wealthy people always earn more income
Income and wealth are the same thing

hook.text: The U.S. poverty line was invented in 1963 based on food costs times three. The formula hasn't changed since.

buildup.text: Mollie Orshansky created the threshold: a minimum food budget multiplied by three, adjusted for family size.

discovery.text: In the 1960s, food was a third of expenses. Today, housing and healthcare dominate. The formula is outdated.

twist.text: Millions living just above the line struggle as much as those below it. The line creates a false binary.

climax.text: The Supplemental Poverty Measure accounts for more costs and benefits but still can't capture lived hardship fully.

punchline.text: The poverty line measures something. Just not actual poverty.

quiz.question: What is the main criticism of the U.S. poverty line?
options: It's based on outdated 1960s spending patterns
It's too generous and overestimates poverty
It was never used by the government

hook.text: The median white family holds eight times the wealth of the median Black family. That gap didn't happen by accident.

buildup.text: Slavery, Jim Crow, redlining, and discriminatory lending systematically blocked Black wealth accumulation for centuries.

discovery.text: The GI Bill gave white veterans homes and college while largely excluding Black veterans from the same benefits.

twist.text: Equal income doesn't produce equal wealth. A Black family earning $100k has far less net worth than a white one.

climax.text: The gap compounds generationally. Without inherited wealth, each generation starts over while others build on a base.

punchline.text: The gap wasn't created by choices. It was built by policy.

quiz.question: What contributed to the racial wealth gap?
options: Discriminatory policies like redlining and the GI Bill
Equal access to all government programs
Differences in personal savings habits only

hook.text: A nurse loses her apartment after one medical bill. She's not an addict or a dropout. She's just broke.

buildup.text: The stereotype of homelessness is addiction and mental illness. The data says the top cause is unaffordable housing.

discovery.text: When housing costs exceed 50% of income, one emergency — a job loss, a medical bill — pushes families to the street.

twist.text: Many homeless people have jobs. The working homeless hold steady employment but can't afford rent in their cities.

climax.text: Cities criminalize sleeping outside rather than building affordable housing. The cheapest policy fails the most people.

punchline.text: Homelessness is a housing problem, not a character flaw.

quiz.question: What is the leading cause of homelessness?
options: Unaffordable housing costs
Substance abuse alone
A lack of personal motivation

hook.text: In Baltimore, life expectancy drops 20 years between two neighborhoods six miles apart. Poverty is a health crisis.

buildup.text: Poor communities have fewer hospitals, worse air quality, limited healthy food, and higher exposure to violence.

discovery.text: Chronic stress from poverty triggers cortisol overload, raising the risk of heart disease, diabetes, and depression.

twist.text: Universal healthcare helps, but it can't fix toxic environments, food deserts, and the daily grind of financial strain.

climax.text: Health inequality is stratification made physical. Your body keeps score of the social position you occupy.

punchline.text: Poverty doesn't just empty wallets. It shortens lives.

quiz.question: How does poverty affect health?
options: Chronic stress and worse environments raise disease risk
Poverty has no measurable effect on health
Only mental health is affected by poverty

hook.text: She worked three jobs, got straight A's, and still couldn't afford college. Meritocracy promises what it can't deliver.

buildup.text: Meritocracy says talent and effort determine success. It's the foundational belief of most modern societies.

discovery.text: Research shows parental income predicts a child's earnings better than grades, test scores, or work ethic.

twist.text: Believing in meritocracy makes people harsher toward the poor. If success is earned, failure must be deserved.

climax.text: Meritocracy isn't just inaccurate — it's harmful. It turns structural failure into personal shame.

punchline.text: Meritocracy works best for those who already have advantages.

quiz.question: What do studies show about meritocracy?
options: Parental income predicts success better than talent or effort
Hard work is the only factor in success
Meritocracy works equally for everyone

hook.text: A college degree used to guarantee a middle-class life. Now it guarantees debt and a fighting chance.

buildup.text: Education is the most studied path to upward mobility. Degrees still boost earnings, but the premium is uneven.

discovery.text: Children from wealthy families are far more likely to attend elite colleges, even with equal academic ability.

twist.text: First-generation students face hidden barriers: no guidance on applications, less financial aid, weaker networks.

climax.text: Education can be an equalizer or a reproducer of inequality, depending on who gets access to quality schools.

punchline.text: Education opens doors — but not the same doors for everyone.

quiz.question: How does education relate to social mobility?
options: It can equalize or reproduce inequality depending on access
It guarantees upward mobility for everyone equally
It has no effect on social position

hook.text: Two children born the same year in the same city: one grows up in a safe suburb, the other in a disinvested block.

buildup.text: Neighborhood shapes exposure to violence, school quality, environmental toxins, and job access from birth.

discovery.text: Raj Chetty's research found that moving to a better neighborhood before age 13 raises lifetime earnings by 31%.

twist.text: But moving is expensive and disruptive. Improving neighborhoods in place may work better than relocating families.

climax.text: Your address at birth is one of the strongest predictors of your adult income, health, and even life expectancy.

punchline.text: Where you grow up shapes who you become. That's the data.

quiz.question: What did Chetty's research show about neighborhoods?
options: Moving to better neighborhoods before age 13 raises earnings
Neighborhood has no effect on adult outcomes
Only rural neighborhoods affect mobility

hook.text: Two equally talented graduates apply for a job. One has an uncle in the company. Guess who gets hired.

buildup.text: Social capital is the value embedded in relationships: who you know, who trusts you, who will vouch for you.

discovery.text: Bourdieu argued that social capital reproduces class. Elite networks pass chances to insiders and shut others out.

twist.text: Social capital is invisible wealth. It doesn't show up on tax returns but shapes outcomes just as powerfully.

climax.text: Building social capital requires access: the right schools, clubs, and events. Access itself is unevenly distributed.

punchline.text: It's not just what you know. It's who knows you back.

quiz.question: What is social capital?
options: The value embedded in social relationships and networks
Money saved in a bank account
The total population of a society

hook.text: She's qualified, experienced, and passed over again. The promotion went to a less experienced man. She's not surprised.

buildup.text: The glass ceiling is an invisible barrier preventing women and minorities from reaching top organizational positions.

discovery.text: Women hold under 10% of Fortune 500 CEO roles. The numbers are worse for women of color at every executive level.

twist.text: The ceiling isn't one barrier. It's a web of bias in hiring, mentoring, networking, and performance evaluation.

climax.text: Token appointments at the top don't break the pattern. Systemic change requires restructuring how organizations work.

punchline.text: The ceiling is invisible to those who've never hit it.

quiz.question: What is the glass ceiling?
options: An invisible barrier blocking women and minorities from top roles
A physical architectural feature in offices
A government regulation limiting promotions

hook.text: A child born in Norway will live 30 years longer than one born in Sierra Leone. Same planet, vastly different fates.

buildup.text: Global inequality is the gap in wealth, health, and opportunity between nations. It's staggering in scale.

discovery.text: The richest 20% of the world's population consumes over 75% of its resources. The poorest 20% gets about 1.5%.

twist.text: Colonialism extracted wealth from Africa and Asia for centuries. Today's map of poverty follows old colonial borders.

climax.text: Global inequality isn't natural. It's the product of conquest, exploitation, and trade rules set by powerful nations.

punchline.text: The global order wasn't designed to be fair. It was designed to be profitable.

quiz.question: What historical factor shaped today's global inequality?
options: Colonialism extracted wealth for centuries
All nations developed independently
Global inequality appeared only recently

hook.text: Belgium's King Leopold killed ten million Congolese to extract rubber. The Congo is still one of the poorest nations.

buildup.text: Colonialism didn't just take resources. It wrecked local institutions, imposed borders, and created dependency.

discovery.text: Colonial powers drew borders that split ethnic groups and merged rivals, seeding conflicts that persist today.

twist.text: Independence didn't reset the clock. Former colonies inherited debt, broken institutions, and unfair trade deals.

climax.text: Neocolonialism continues through debt structures, resource extraction, and trade agreements written by wealthy nations.

punchline.text: The colonies became nations. The exploitation just changed form.

quiz.question: How does colonialism affect modern inequality?
options: It destroyed institutions and created lasting dependency
It had no long-term effects on former colonies
It benefited all countries equally

hook.text: Your $20 shirt was sewn by a woman in Bangladesh earning $3 a day. Global supply chains connect comfort to suffering.

buildup.text: Corporations move production to countries with cheap labor and weak regulations to maximize profit.

discovery.text: The Rana Plaza factory collapse in 2013 killed 1,134 workers. Safety warnings had been ignored by owners and brands.

twist.text: Some economists argue sweatshops are still better than local alternatives. Workers often disagree when asked directly.

climax.text: The real issue isn't whether these jobs exist, but who profits most and who bears all the risk.

punchline.text: Cheap goods have a price. Someone else is paying it.

quiz.question: What does the Rana Plaza disaster reveal about global labor?
options: Worker safety is sacrificed for corporate profit
Sweatshops are well-regulated worldwide
Global supply chains benefit workers equally

hook.text: Billions in aid flow to Africa every year. Poverty barely budges. Is aid helping, hurting, or missing the point?

buildup.text: Foreign aid was designed to lift poor nations. Jeffrey Sachs argued that massive aid could end extreme poverty.

discovery.text: Dambisa Moyo countered that aid creates dependency, fuels corruption, and undermines local businesses and governance.

twist.text: Randomized trials show some aid works well — bed nets reduce malaria deaths. But systemic poverty needs systemic fixes.

climax.text: The debate isn't aid or no aid. It's what kind, who controls it, and whether it addresses root causes.

punchline.text: Aid can treat symptoms. Only justice treats the disease.

quiz.question: What is a key criticism of foreign aid?
options: It can create dependency and undermine local governance
It always eliminates poverty effectively
It is never useful in any context

hook.text: He crossed a desert to pick fruit for minimum wage. Back home, that money feeds his entire family for a month.

buildup.text: Migration is driven by global inequality. People move from poor regions to rich ones seeking opportunity.

discovery.text: Remittances — money sent home by migrants — exceed all foreign aid combined. Migration is a survival strategy.

twist.text: Receiving countries benefit from cheap labor but restrict migrants' rights. They want the work without the worker.

climax.text: Migration doesn't erase inequality. It redistributes it. Migrants gain income but lose community, safety, and status.

punchline.text: People don't leave home for fun. They leave because they must.

quiz.question: How do remittances compare to foreign aid?
options: Remittances exceed all foreign aid combined
Foreign aid is much larger than remittances
They are roughly equal in size

hook.text: A surgeon earns more than a janitor. Functionalists say that's necessary — society rewards the jobs it needs most.

buildup.text: Davis and Moore argued in 1945 that inequality motivates people to fill important, difficult positions.

discovery.text: Their logic: without higher rewards, no one would endure years of training to become a doctor or engineer.

twist.text: Critics ask: who decides which jobs are important? Teachers and nurses are essential but poorly paid.

climax.text: Functionalism explains some inequality but ignores power: the wealthy shape which positions get rewarded and how.

punchline.text: Rewards don't follow importance. They follow power.

quiz.question: What does functionalist theory claim about inequality?
options: It motivates people to fill important positions
It is always harmful and unnecessary
It is caused entirely by discrimination

hook.text: The rules of the game were written by the winners. Conflict theory says inequality exists because the powerful want it.

buildup.text: Rooted in Marx, conflict theory argues that the wealthy use their power to maintain their advantages.

discovery.text: Laws, schools, and media all serve ruling-class interests — often invisibly, by making inequality seem natural.

twist.text: Functionalists see consensus. Conflict theorists see domination dressed as consensus. Both have evidence.

climax.text: Conflict theory explains why inequality persists even when most people oppose it: power structures resist change.

punchline.text: Inequality persists not by accident, but by design.

quiz.question: What does conflict theory say about inequality?
options: The powerful maintain it to protect their advantages
It naturally motivates hard work
It affects everyone equally

hook.text: A respected pastor earns little money but commands a room. Weber said stratification isn't just about wealth.

buildup.text: Weber proposed three dimensions of stratification: class (wealth), status (prestige), and party (political power).

discovery.text: These dimensions overlap but don't always align. A professor has prestige but less wealth than a business owner.

twist.text: Marx reduced everything to economics. Weber showed that honor and political influence create separate hierarchies.

climax.text: Modern sociology uses all three dimensions to map inequality more accurately than any single measure allows.

punchline.text: Inequality isn't one ladder. It's three, and they don't line up.

quiz.question: What are Weber's three dimensions of stratification?
options: Class, status, and party
Income, race, and gender
Education, religion, and age

hook.text: Two students ace the exam. One knows which fork to use at a business dinner. Guess who gets the internship.

buildup.text: Pierre Bourdieu argued that class isn't only about money. Knowledge, tastes, and manners are also capital.

discovery.text: Cultural capital includes education, accent, taste in art, and knowing social codes — all learned from your class.

twist.text: Schools reward cultural capital they don't teach. Kids from educated homes arrive with advantages that look like talent.

climax.text: Bourdieu showed that inequality hides inside culture. It reproduces through taste, manners, and invisible social codes.

punchline.text: Class isn't just in your wallet. It's in your accent and tastes.

quiz.question: What is cultural capital?
options: Knowledge, tastes, and social skills learned from one's class
Money invested in cultural events
The number of books in a library

hook.text: Scandinavian countries have half the inequality of the U.S. and higher happiness. The tradeoff isn't what you'd expect.

buildup.text: Progressive taxation, universal healthcare, and free education are proven tools for reducing inequality.

discovery.text: Nordic nations show that lower inequality correlates with better health, less crime, and stronger economic growth.

twist.text: Political will is the barrier, not knowledge. We know what works. Implementing it threatens powerful interests.

climax.text: Reducing inequality is a choice societies make — or refuse to make. The tools exist. The question is power.

punchline.text: We know how to reduce inequality. The question is: who decides?

quiz.question: What do Nordic countries demonstrate about inequality?
options: Lower inequality correlates with better health and growth
Inequality is impossible to reduce
High inequality leads to more innovation
